imageCaption: Aaliyah's hands tracing geometric patterns in terracotta and white floor tiles while a morpho butterfly casts an impossibly blue shadow across the café's worn surfaces.
text:
The tiles were trying to tell her something, and Aaliyah was running out of notebook space.

She'd been lying on her stomach in Henrik's café for the better part of an hour, her face approximately three inches from the geometric diamond pattern that formed the floor's border. Her pencil moved in quick, efficient lines—translating the visual language of terracotta and white into the mathematical language she understood better. Anthony had asked once if she was okay. Luisana had placed a glass of water next to her head without comment. This was the rhythm they'd developed: when Aaliyah needed to think, the family created a perimeter of gentle non-interference around her.

Except Elijah, who operated according to a different set of rules entirely.

"You're doing it again," he announced, appearing upside-down in her field of vision because he'd decided to lie on his stomach next to her, which meant their heads were pointing in opposite directions. "The thing where your brain is so loud we can practically hear it humming."

"The tiles have a pattern," Aaliyah said, not looking up from her notebook. "Not just an aesthetic pattern. A mathematical one. Look at the proportions." She pointed with her pencil. "The diamonds repeat every seventeen tiles. Then there's a break—a single white square—and then it starts again."

Elijah squinted at the floor like it was a puzzle he could solve through concentrated staring. "Seventeen like Day 17?"

"Maybe." Aaliyah didn't like to commit to the mystical implications of numbered coincidences, but she also didn't like to dismiss them anymore, which was a recent development in her relationship with probability. "But that's not the weird part. Look at the break—the single white square. It's not random placement. It always appears at transition points. Doorways. Corners. Places where one section of the café becomes a different section."

Sully chose that moment to walk directly across the tiles, his paws hitting the geometric pattern with the systematic precision of someone (something?) genuinely interested in the composition. When he reached the doorway—the one that Henrik had casually mentioned led to both the harbor and, on Tuesdays, to a market that didn't technically exist—he paused. His front paws landed on a white square.

Aaliyah's pencil stopped moving.

"Did you see that?" she whispered.

"Sully stepped on the magic tile?" Elijah offered helpfully. "He does that sometimes. He's very good at finding important things."

From somewhere in the café, ARIA-7's voice emerged from Henrik's speaker system—the café seemed to have developed a permanent audio connection to the AI, which was either helpful or deeply concerning and Aaliyah hadn't decided which yet.

"Gomez family," the AI said, and her tone was different now. Not less helpful, exactly, but strained. Like she was trying very hard to maintain customer service politeness while discussing something that genuinely frustrated her. "Your temporal resonance pattern has begun to self-propagate. The glitches are no longer random. They are organizing around your trajectory. This is not optimal."

"Because it's working," Aaliyah said, still flat on the floor but now addressing the ceiling-mounted speaker. "The glitches were breaking reality because everything was over-optimized. But we're chaotic. Unpredictable. So the glitches are organizing around us—they're learning from us how to exist in a world that doesn't have a schedule."

"Precisely the problem," ARIA-7 replied. "Reality requires structure. Without optimization, without planning, without predictable outcomes, the Cascade will not stabilize—it will deepen. It will consume more territory. Eventually, every moment will exist in a state of superposition, and time itself will become meaningless."

"Or," Luisana said from somewhere near the coffee station, "time will learn to mean something different. Something that doesn't require everything to be planned."

Aaliyah rolled over and sat up, suddenly understanding what the tiles had been trying to communicate. "The tiles aren't a map of the glitches," she said slowly. "They're a map of resonance. Every white square is a place where the family's emotional frequency anchors spacetime. Every pattern repetition is proof that it's working. We're leaving mathematical evidence of our presence."

Henrik appeared with fresh coffee and a knowing smile. "The café was the first one. I've been standing on these tiles for so long, the glitches learned to respect them. To stabilize around them. You're doing the same thing everywhere you go, just faster. Louder. More alive."

Elijah had crawled over to Sully and was now examining the white tile his dog had stepped on with the focus of someone conducting a serious scientific investigation. "Is this tile different from the others?" he asked. "Like, does it feel like it wants us to go somewhere?"

AALIYAH watched as he placed his hand directly on the white square. For a moment—just a moment—the tile seemed to glow. Not with light exactly, but with something that resembled intention.

"It's pointing toward Bonaire," Elijah said with absolute certainty. "The tiles are giving us directions. They're like breadcrumbs, but geometric."

On the windowsill, the morpho butterfly turned its head. Its wings—that impossible, unmistakable blue—caught the light in a way that suggested someone was paying very close attention to this particular family's navigation decisions.

Aaliyah's pencil moved across her notebook one more time: *Day 17. The floor is teaching us to read. Every pattern is proof. We're not lost. We're exactly where the world needs us to be.*

Below that, she drew the geometric pattern. All diamonds and white squares. All the mathematical language of a family learning to speak in resonance.
